---
title: "Bayesian Ordinal Regression for Imaging Scores"
description: "Why ordinal models matter for radiology severity grading and how Bayesian inference helps"
date: 2025-08-01
categories: [Bayesian, statistics, methodology]
draft : true
---

::: {.note-meta}
🌱 Seedling · Last tended <span style="background:#fff3cd;color:#856404;padding:0.15em 0.5em;border-radius:3px;font-size:0.9em;border:1px dashed #ffc107;">?? Month Year</span>
:::

## The intuition

Many radiology scores are ordinal — severity grades like 0/1/2/3 where the distances between categories aren't necessarily equal. Treating these as continuous (linear regression) or collapsing them to binary (logistic regression) throws away information. Ordinal models respect the ranked structure.

## Why Bayesian?

<span style="background:#fff3cd;color:#856404;padding:0.15em 0.5em;border-radius:3px;font-size:0.9em;border:1px dashed #ffc107;">?? Explain the advantages of Bayesian ordinal models for your specific context: uncertainty quantification, small sample sizes typical of imaging studies, incorporating prior clinical knowledge, posterior predictive checks.</span>

## Key references

<span style="background:#fff3cd;color:#856404;padding:0.15em 0.5em;border-radius:3px;font-size:0.9em;border:1px dashed #ffc107;">?? List 3-5 key papers or textbooks you've found useful. Brief annotation for each.</span>

## How I use this

<span style="background:#fff3cd;color:#856404;padding:0.15em 0.5em;border-radius:3px;font-size:0.9em;border:1px dashed #ffc107;">?? Connect to your own work — the ESGAR abstract, the JCC manuscript. What specific modeling choices did you make and why?</span>

## Related

- <span style="background:#fff3cd;color:#856404;padding:0.15em 0.5em;border-radius:3px;font-size:0.9em;border:1px dashed #ffc107;">?? Link to relevant paper notes</span>
- <span style="background:#fff3cd;color:#856404;padding:0.15em 0.5em;border-radius:3px;font-size:0.9em;border:1px dashed #ffc107;">?? Link to a methods note on BRMS or Stan if you write one</span>
